inline_comments.message: Question: This exception is closely coupled to the JAXRS response code, so in a REST context, in principle seems to me that ControlLoopException is general enough, have doubts if it has to carry the REST response http code, may be move it to a subclass ?
how do these fields get populated if there's no constructors?   Perhaps there are side effects with the filter operations on the stream processing below?
Should these be final as only settable in constructor?
umm I thought that we usually would get checkstyle errors here if missing javadoc at the start of public class .. (?)
not sure if this class is a;most identical to ControlLoopFilter before .. one possibility to reuse same code would be to template the Participant if that was the case.
Yes, Added. BTW we don't have license headers on our policy types and policy examples. We probably should add them.

This is a copy of the filter types we have for filtering policy types and policies in policy models. The way it's done today, we have to create a new filter and comparator for each type we're working with. I was chatting with Jim about this a few weeks ago and we said we'd look at how we can make these classes general (maybe using generics) to avoid having to create 2 classes for each class we filter.
I'll check this. Its building for us but maybe it shouldn't.

Example in the source code.
If those are examples those files should go under src/test/resources

Do you have some tests to add ??
We have  3K lines added without a single test  :-(

A comparator is generally needed when multiple ordering could be required, in that case multiple comparator classes may be implemented, which seems not to be the case here as you define the "compare" method by using a method of controlLoop class.

I think ControlLoop class should probably does that instead: "implements Comparable<ControlLoop>", this will reduce the code complexity.
Same comment than for controlloop comparator, this class is probably not needed. The code should be moved under the Participant class

I recommend to read this article as i believe you will end up with Hibernate right ?
https://thorben-janssen.com/lombok-hibernate-how-to-avoid-common-pitfalls/

You must be very careful with entity objects, the auto generated code might not be convenient for the hibernate layer (mainly because the equals and hascode should be based on the primary key only, otherwise hibernate could consider in its cache that the objects are different while they are not)
Same comment for lombok annotation defined on Entity objects
Same comment for lombok annotation defined on Entity objects

Just an note: You are not forced to use H2, you can benefit from what we do in clamp, we create a mariadb docker instance for the maven integration tests, it's better to test it with the final DB type :-)

Well they should be available in the resource bundles for other modules to use them. We used this approach in Policy for Policy examples. We have a whole module in there with policy type and policy examples. This works well for us because we then have all unit tests using the same examples so, if we decide to change a format of policy, then everyone is pulling from the same module/resource bundle for tests and the new format gets verified by the existing tests. See
https://github.com/onap/policy-models/tree/master/models-examples

Yes, tests are coming, as this is PoC code, we wanted to push up the code as soon as possible rather than doing a big code dump. In the event the code is merged to master at some point in the future, we will comply with all code coverage and other directives. For example style guides and SONAR is already on.

If ye'd prefer to wait and take everything as the finished article, we can do it that way either, let's discuss at the scrum.

This is a pattern we use for filters in the Policy Framework on model classes, We know this is not great, Jim and I are discussing how to make this generic, see similar classes in this package.

Today we have to create a specific filter class and a comparator class for every concept we want to filter on and that's not good, but this should be fixed in policy-models.

https://github.com/onap/policy-models/tree/master/models-tosca/src/main/java/org/onap/policy/models/tosca/authorative/concepts

We haven't used Hibernate in the Policy Framework, I think because it had security issues if I remember correctly. Again, this is following the pattern for persistence classes we're using in the Policy Framework. See:
https://github.com/onap/policy-models/tree/master/models-tosca/src/main/java/org/onap/policy/models/tosca/simple/concepts

We ahve a whole framework for persisting and reading TOSCA artifacts that uses EclipseLink. It works well. Now we are discussing in the PF how to move to predefined tables but for now we haven't worked through that and it'll probably come in Istanbul so for now I followed the current way of doing things.
Yes, I agree, it would be better to use MariaDB. We should discuss this and see how we can bring MariaDB into the Policy Framework for unit tests using your approach.

I thought about this too, My thinking here was that it was worth dragging in the JAXRS response code even in cases where it is not a REST exception because the Response.Status codes can be applied more generally than just in REST and cold maybe help users (and force developers!) to categorize their exceptions.

But maybe it should be in a subclass.

Yes, Jim and I discussed this. We should look at filters on concepts because in the approach we now have we have to create a ....Filter and a ....Comparator class for every class we want to filter on, so we have to create two classes for every one we filter on, see the comparators and filters here:
https://github.com/onap/policy-models/tree/master/models-tosca/src/main/java/org/onap/policy/models/tosca/authorative/concepts

Part of the reason is that you can't subclass a Comparator and have a subclass of a comparator work on its parent, but I'm sure there is some nice way around this.

See also my reply on Seb's comment.
It doesn't seem to give a checkstyle error but I put in a comment anyway.

Yes but i would say that it's a small mistake, we embed some files (sometimes huge, even if they are compressed) to Prod artifact.
I understand very well the need, and there is a specific way to do that with maven, it's possible to create test-jar that will be tagged in test scope when used, that test artifact should be part of the Nexus deliverable:

https://maven.apache.org/plugins/maven-jar-plugin/examples/create-test-jar.html

Did you try that already (even in PEF code) ? I think I did try it in the past, it was working pretty well.

Well i'm not saying it's bad :-) 
I'm just saying it's a useless complexity, unless you have a very good reason to do it. 
Which seems to be the case apparently ? Is this code currently coupled with that policy filter code part (may be i missed it)?

Sorry my bad, it's not only for hibernate, it's for JPA entities in general and any framework you use for the db access.

I believe that this will have to be changed in PEF, the impacts are performance issues with db accesses and sometimes exceptions (like LazyInitializationException in hibernate, etc ...)

Well it's linked in that we're using the same pattern for filtering that's used in the policy-models code. We want to come up with a generic way of avoiding having to create these two classes for all filtering. I'll probably have a go at it when I get a few minutes to look at it.  Ideally we would have a generic filter that we can apply to a concept class, the problem is that if you want to use a comparator, the comparator has to work on a specific class, not a superclass. I'm sure there's a way around it but just need to find a few hours to do it.

I generalised the approach in the policy framework and we can now use generics for filters so the filters and comparators are removed here now.

A control loop filter is now:
ToscaTypedEntityFilter<ControlLoop>

See this review: https://gerrit.onap.org/r/c/policy/models/+/117573

Filters are now generic, see the comment above and this review:
https://gerrit.onap.org/r/c/policy/models/+/117573
See comment above. comparators are now gone.

The idea with the examples is that anything here are useful working examples for use cases that work. We have found this approach very useful in ONAP, I very often refer people with questions to the examples location in Github and with Github they are very accessible.

Filters are now made generic in policy/models. See review:
https://gerrit.onap.org/r/c/policy/models/+/117573

A participant filter is now done like this:
TOscaTyepdEntityFilter<Participant> and the ParticipantFilter class is gone.

Yes i totally agree with you, but why store them into the src/resource instead of src/test/resource, normally this type of examples should not go to production jars, war, etc... This tend to grow the Jars we deliver with useless things inside .
They should be under the src/test/resources and be bundled into a test jar, and they will still be on github/gerrit?

Or may be i missed something

Just a note here because i'm thinking to the issue we currently have in SDC.
As you are the lucky one that begins this project, you can probably divide the tests into several smaller tests (I say that for all tests in general)

This seems totally useless at the beginning but when we have 3 to 4K test classes (like in SDC) it really makes sense to execute them in parallel as you can divide the time required to build the project by 5 or 6 (in terms of methods  not only in terms of test classes), this can be configured in the surefire plugin, and I would really recommend to enable it at the beginning of your project.

So you validate 3 things: 
1. Each test methods within the same class are totally independent
2. No static code used between test classes or within the same class
2. Vertical scaling will at least be useful for the test phase.
No assertion ? do we verify if no exception is raised ?
Same comment than before try to split into smaller tests, it does not cost a lot to do it right now, and it can save your time one day

Strange to test lombok code, not sure it's really useful, it's not going to be part of the coverage anyway.
That's one of the advantage of lombok, it's something we do not have to take care anymore.

Same comment, for me that's useless.
I prefer to see your functionalities tested instead :-)

As i said earlier,
Change the surefire options in the plugin section for all your Tosca code:
            <plugin>
                <groupId>org.apache.maven.plugins</groupId>
                <artifactId>maven-surefire-plugin</artifactId>
                <version>2.22.2</version>
                <configuration>
                    <forkCount>1C</forkCount>
                    <reuseForks>true</reuseForks>
                    <useSystemClassLoader>false</useSystemClassLoader>
                    <parallel>methods</parallel>
                </configuration>
            </plugin>

From the surefire doc:
"When using reuseForks=true and a forkCount value larger than one, test classes are handed over to the forked process one-by-one. Thus, parallel=classes would not change anything. However, you can use parallel=methods: classes are executed in forkCount concurrent processes, each of the processes can then use threadCount threads to execute the methods of one class in parallel."

I want this to throw an exception in those cases. If OrderedState is "cast" to "State" and "State" is in one of it's transition states then that is an error on the server's state handling and should throw an exception.

Yes, if the state is in a transitional state and orderedState is not set, it is an error in the state handling. The only way a state can change is if orderedState is set, so if orderedState is null and state is transitioning there is something very odd happening in the server's state handling.
OrderedState marked @NonNull now so this can "never" be null (according to SONAR) but it can actually be null when the input JSON/YAML is incorrect. We will validate the JSON/YAML on input.
This was not flagged by SONAR in my eclipse but I changed it anyway to be more readable.

This is the way the OpenPojo validation works. It does assertions and throws exceptions internally, there is no "assert" in the test case. see for example:

https://github.com/OpenPojo/openpojo

SONAR seems to know about this because it does not flag a "no assert" issue on this test case.

Yes, if the state is in a transitional state and orderedState is not set, it is an error in the state handling. The only way a state can change is if orderedState is set, so if orderedState is null and state is transitioning there is something very odd happening in the server's state handling.
OrderedState marked @NonNull now so this can "never" be null (according to SONAR) but it can actually be null when the input JSON/YAML is incorrect. We will validate the JSON/YAML on input.

Not a comment for this review but my main concern in the near future with having all these programmatically generated database table databases is how to do the data migration from release to release when there is schema changes.  Not having a good strategy, would be a problem for adoption of the software, as users would want to keep their data from release to release seemingly.

Yes this sounds like a good approach, we will make a test jar using the suggested method above.

I have opened a sub task on our Infrastructure/Integration story for this:
https://jira.onap.org/browse/POLICY-3064
Yes, I agree, we need to address this in the overall project and cascade it to here if a decision is made to continue with this work in Istanbul.
